document: Directive in respect of cybersecurity and cyber-resilisnce within the national payment system

sections.heading: Definitions
sections.title: Definitions of critical cybersecurity terms for payment systems
sections.summary: This section defines key terms specific to payment systems and cybersecurity, including critical service providers, cyber-attacks, cyber-incidents, cyber-resilience, cybersecurity, cyber-threats, information assets, information systems, information technology systems, multi-factor authentication, payment institutions, payment system financial market infrastructure, operators, senior management, sensitive information, and vulnerability assessments. These definitions establish the foundational language for the directive's cybersecurity and cyber-resilience requirements.

requirements.text: Critical service provider means a service provider that provides critical services to a payment institution in the NPS
Compromise means a violation of the security of an information technology (IT) system
Cyber-attack means malicious attempt(s) to exploit vulnerabilities through the cyber medium to damage, disrupt or gain unauthorised access to assets
Cyber-event means any observable occurrence in an information system
Cyber-incident means a cyber-event that adversely affects the cybersecurity of an information system and/or the information that the system processes, stores or transmits
Cyber-resilience means the ability of a payment institution or an operator to continue carrying out its mission by anticipating and adapting to cyber-threats
Cyber-risk means the combination of the probability of cyber-incidents occurring and their impact
Cybersecurity means the preservation of confidentiality, integrity and availability of information and/or information systems through the cyber-medium
Cyber-threat means a circumstance with the potential to exploit one or more vulnerabilities that adversely affects cybersecurity
Information asset means any piece of data, device or other component of the environment that supports information-related activities
Information system means a set of applications, services, information technology assets or other information-handling components
Information technology system means a set of hardware, software, network or other information technology components
Multi-factor authentication means the use of two or more authentication factors to verify the identity of a user or system
Payment institution means persons designated, authorised, registered or regulated under the National Payment System Act 78 of 1998
Payment system Financial Market Infrastructure means a multilateral system among payment system participants
Operator means an operator of a payment system, including payment clearing house system operators
Senior management means the chief executive officer or the person in charge of a payment institution
Sensitive information means information where loss, misuse or unauthorised access could adversely affect the public interest
Vulnerability assessment means a systematic examination of an IT system to determine the adequacy of security measures

sections.heading: Background
sections.title: Background on payment system cybersecurity risks
sections.summary: This section provides context on the South African Reserve Bank's (SARB) role in payment system regulation, the scope of the national payment system (NPS), and the increasing cyber-risks posed by digitization and fintech. It highlights how cyber-attacks on payment systems could disrupt the NPS, leading to financial instability and systemic events, and emphasizes the need for cybersecurity and cyber-resilience measures to protect payment institutions and systems.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.title: Purpose of cybersecurity directive for payment systems
sections.summary: This section states that the South African Reserve Bank (SARB) issues the directive to impose cybersecurity and cyber-resilience requirements on payment institutions, payment, clearing and settlement systems, payment system financial market infrastructures (FMIs), and operators within the national payment system (NPS). The purpose is to ensure the safety, efficiency, and resilience of the NPS through robust cybersecurity measures.

sections.heading: Directive
sections.title: Cybersecurity and cyber-resilience requirements for payment systems
sections.summary: This section mandates payment institutions and operators to develop and maintain cybersecurity and cyber-resilience frameworks that include cyber-governance, identification of critical operations and information assets, cybersecurity measures, detection mechanisms, and response and recovery protocols. These frameworks must ensure the protection of critical systems, timely incident response, and adherence to recovery timelines to maintain payment system stability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes critical requirements for data transfer impact assessments, including the need to identify critical information assets, implement robust cybersecurity measures, and establish response protocols that ensure timely recovery from cyber-attacks to prevent systemic disruptions in payment systems.

requirements.text: Payment institutions and operators must develop and maintain cybersecurity and cyber-resilience frameworks
Payment institutions and operators must have written effective cyber-governance arrangements
Board or senior management must determine cyber-risk tolerance levels
Board or senior management must approve cybersecurity policies and strategy
Board or senior management must oversee development and implementation of cybersecurity frameworks
Board or senior management must ensure annual review of cybersecurity policies
Cybersecurity framework must be aligned with operational risk management
Cybersecurity framework must be based on industry standards and best practices
Cybersecurity framework must articulate identification of cyber-risks and controls
Board or senior management must appoint a senior executive accountable for cybersecurity
Cybersecurity framework must include information sharing with SARB and stakeholders
Senior management must keep board informed on cybersecurity status
Senior management must ensure compliance with this directive
Senior management must conduct due diligence on entities introduced to NPS
Payment institutions must conduct third-party risk assessments annually
Roles and responsibilities for cybersecurity must be clearly outlined in agreements
Payment institutions must identify critical technology, operations, and information assets
Payment institutions must identify internal processes and external dependencies for security
Payment institutions must ensure cybersecurity frameworks protect confidentiality, integrity and availability
Payment institutions must embed protective controls to minimize cyber-attack impact
Payment institutions must develop measures to protect critical and sensitive information
Payment institutions must develop measures to mitigate risks from interconnectedness
Payment institutions must develop measures to address anomalous staff behavior
Payment institutions must train staff on cyber-risk detection and response
Payment institutions must design cyber-secure payment instruments and services
Payment institutions must implement cyber-hygiene measures including strong password security
Payment institutions must implement multi-factor authentication for critical systems
Payment institutions must ensure network perimeter defense controls
Payment institutions must implement multiple-layer security controls
Payment institutions must ensure security patches are applied
Payment institutions must test security patches for compatibility
Payment institutions must ensure security standards for software, systems and devices
Payment institutions must monitor application of security standards
Payment institutions must implement malware protection
Payment institutions must scan information assets for malicious activities
Payment institutions must ensure data loss prevention policies
Payment institutions must encrypt data storage systems and endpoint devices
Payment institutions must ensure third-party managed systems are protected
Payment institutions must ensure cyber-attack trigger points and detection measures
Payment institutions must implement multi-layered trigger indicators for detection
Payment institutions must identify irregular behavior by persons with access
Payment institutions must have arrangements for resumption of critical operations
Payment institutions must detect cyber-attack attempts and successful attacks
Payment institutions must initiate recovery efforts upon successful cyber-attack
Payment institutions must have incident response processes for recovery
Payment institutions must have recovery measures within 2 hours for payment systems

sections.heading: Directive (cont.)
sections.title: Cyber-resilience planning and testing for payment institutions
sections.summary: Payment institutions must develop and test cyber-resilience plans quarterly, update them using threat intelligence and past incidents, include third-party management in their frameworks, and ensure cloud compliance with SARB regulations. These requirements apply to payment institutions and operators to maintain critical system resilience during cyber incidents.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires payment institutions to maintain robust cyber-resilience frameworks with third-party and cloud service providers, including specific testing and monitoring protocols that impact data transfer and storage security across borders.

requirements.text: Develop and test response, resumption and recovery plans quarterly
Update plans based on threat intelligence and past cyber-events
Include third-party management plans in cyber-resilience frameworks
Conduct extensive due diligence on third-party cyber-resilience measures
Assess criticality of outsourced processes before contracts
Obtain independent security attestation reports from third parties
Implement and test business continuity plans with third parties
Ensure compliance with SARB cloud computing and data offshoring regulations
Identify, monitor and mitigate jurisdiction risks for cloud data
Remain accountable for data stored and processed on cloud
Develop and implement cyber-resilience testing programmes
Simulate cyber-attacks to identify vulnerabilities
Conduct internal and external penetration testing
Test systems after significant changes for security vulnerabilities
Perform regular vulnerability assessments
Share cyber threat and risk information with regulators and trusted parties
Plan information sharing through trusted channels
Participate in information-sharing groups like Cybersecurity Hub
Ensure client data protection during information sharing
Comply with Cybercrimes Act regarding information disclosure
Understand cyber-threat landscape and risk mitigation adequacy
Develop cyber-threat intelligence processes
Collect information about cyber-threats from other payment institutions
Ensure cybersecurity frameworks adapt to evolving cyber-risk
Learn from previous cyber-incidents to improve security systems
Monitor technological developments to counter cyber-attacks
Implement predictive capabilities for proactive cyber-protection
Report material cyber-incidents to SARB within 24 hours
Provide detailed incident reports to SARB within 48 hours

sections.source_quote: Payment institutions or operators must develop and implement cyber-resilience testing programmes and methodologies which include the following: different test scenarios and simulations of various cyber-attacks

sections.heading: Supervision and compliance monitoring
sections.title: SARB supervision and inspection powers for payment institutions
sections.summary: The SARB may conduct unscheduled inspections of payment institutions with prior written notice, requiring inspectors to present identification and authority documents. Inspectors can access premises, documents, and systems without consent under specific conditions, including warrants for entry. Payment institutions must provide signed receipts for documents taken during inspections.
sections.dtia_summary: SARB's inspection powers allow for data access and system checks that impact data transfer and storage compliance, particularly for financial institutions operating across borders.

requirements.text: SARB may conduct onsite or offsite inspections to promote compliance
SARB must provide written notification before onsite inspections
Inspection notification specifies dates, SARB representatives, review period, and required documentation
SARB representatives must produce letter of authority and identity documents
SARB may enter business premises without consent
SARB may enter private residences with consent if business is conducted there
SARB may enter premises without notice if warrant is issued
SARB may enter premises with senior staff authority if warrant is likely
SARB may enter premises to conduct inspections without notice if necessary
SARB representatives have right to access any part of premises and documents
SARB may open strongrooms to access relevant documents
SARB may examine, copy, and make extracts from documents
SARB may question individuals for relevant information
SARB may require production of documents relevant to inspection
SARB may operate computer systems to search information
SARB may take copies of documents as evidence of contravention
SARB must provide signed receipt for documents taken
Payment institutions may examine documents under SARB supervision
Individuals may object to answering questions or producing documents
SARB may require answers or documents despite objections
Magistrates may issue warrants for inspections if conditions are met
Warrants must be signed by issuing judge or magistrate
SARB must fix warrant copy on premises if no one is in charge
SARB must produce warrant on demand

sections.source_quote: The SARB may at any time conduct a supervisory onsite or offsite inspection on payment institutions or operators, in a form and manner that the SARB may determine, to promote compliance with this directive.

sections.heading: Effective date and non-compliance
sections.title: Effective date and non-compliance penalties for payment institutions
sections.summary: This directive takes effect three months after publication, with SARB retaining amendment rights. Payment institutions must comply with all requirements, and contraventions are offences under the NPS Act, subject to penalties.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets the effective date and legal consequences for non-compliance, directly impacting data transfer and processing compliance timelines and penalties for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Directive effective three months after publication
SARB may amend requirements in the directive
Payment institutions must comply with directive requirements
Contravention of directive is an offence under NPS Act

sections.source_quote: This directive will be effective three months after its publication. The SARB reserves the right to amend any requirements in this directive.
